evidence: main composition in `main.go` directly initializes and boots subsystems (`broadcast.Initialize`, `docker.Initialize`, `importer.Initialize`, `routines.CheckVersionLoop`, `routines.DockerListenerWithContext`, `rectify.*Handler`, `leak.StartLeak`) without a dedicated orchestration abstraction.

Multiple modules expose or depend on package-global seam/function vars (`isConfigReady`, `emitToLeak`, and `schedulePackBus` in `broadcast/broadcast.go`; `dockerClientNew`, `containerConfigResolver`, etc. in `docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go`; `setUploadResponseHeaders`/session state in `importer/importer.go`), which couples tests and callers to mutable global behavior.

Runtime seams are heavily duplicated as ad-hoc function bundles in `routines/runtime_seams.go` and `docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`, with no-op/default closures defined inline (`createDefaultWGConfFn: func() error { return nil }`, etc.) that hide explicit contract boundaries.

A single request path is doing full orchestration, state mutation, I/O, and transport responsibilities: `goseg/handler/system/support.go:SupportHandler` and `goseg/importer/importer.go:HTTPUploadHandler` both parse inbound payloads, perform lifecycle/session updates, run multi-step workflows, and emit side effects (state transitions, file writes, external calls, status publishing) in one function body.

Dependency seams are implemented as many mutable package-level function vars in multiple modules (for example `goseg/system/disk.go`, `goseg/system/wifi.go`, `goseg/config/mc.go`, `goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`, and `goseg/shipworkflow/startram.go`), creating a repeated cross-cutting pattern rather than a small, explicit abstraction boundary.

findings.summary: `main.go` mixes process orchestration policy, subsystem lifecycle sequencing, configuration control flow, and long-running service startup in one function, making composition hard to reason about and weakly abstracted.
evidence: `main.go` `main()` directly performs initialization (`config.Initialize`, `broadcast.Initialize`, `docker.Initialize`) and immediately starts many background workers (`CheckVersionLoop`, `AptUpdateLoop`, `DockerListenerWithContext`, multiple rectify handlers, leak/startup loops).

CLI argument handling and runtime behavior (`--http-port`, `dev`) are coupled with boot orchestration and scheduling decisions (`killSwitch`, version update path, WG/MinIO/Llama load paths) in the same unit.
No clear orchestrator abstraction exists to declare startup dependencies and shutdown ordering; orchestration is implicit in procedural code.
findings.suggestion: Introduce an explicit `Bootstrap`/`RuntimeCoordinator` component with a lifecycle interface that owns startup graph ordering, dependency readiness barriers, and policy flags; keep `main()` as a thin argument parser that composes components and invokes the coordinator.

findings.summary: Auth state management is exposed as mutable package-global state (`ClientManager`), forcing other modules to depend on global variables instead of stable interfaces.
evidence: `auth.go` declares exported mutable singleton `var ClientManager = NewClientManager()`.
`broadcast/broadcast.go` writes through `auth.ClientManager.BroadcastAuth` and `.BroadcastUnauth` directly rather than via an injected abstraction.
`importer/importer.go` uses `auth.TokenIdAuthed(auth.ClientManager, token.ID)` instead of an auth service interface.
findings.suggestion: Encapsulate client manager behind an internal interface (e.g., `AuthBus`) and inject it into consumers; keep `ClientManager` unexported or readonly, expose constructor/setter hooks for tests, and use getter methods everywhere to avoid global variable coupling.

findings.summary: Runtime indirection is layered repeatedly through function field bundles and mutable defaults, creating high indirection cost with limited abstraction payoff.
evidence: `routines/runtime_seams.go` defines multiple small function-injected structs for nearly every interaction (`dockerRoutineContainerOps`, `dockerRoutineBroadcastOps`, `dockerRoutineShipOps`, `runtimeBase` wrappers).

`docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` adds analogous runtime structs and no-op defaults (`createDefaultWGConfFn`, `getWgConfFn`) in `newDockerRuntime`, overriding behavior only in narrow constructors like `newWireguardRuntime`.
Lifecycle seams in `docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go` expose global mutable function hooks (`SetImagePuller`, `SetContainerConfigResolver`) plus package-level state used to alter behavior externally.
findings.suggestion: Replace repeated single-purpose closure bundles with explicit interfaces for narrow ports (e.g., container lifecycle, config snapshot, broadcast transport) and concrete implementations, reducing layers of pass-through indirection and making seam ownership explicit.

findings.summary: Static, versioned domain data is embedded as large inline structs/JSON strings, making release and policy updates coupled to recompilation and weakening abstraction boundaries between defaults and behavior.
evidence: `defaults/defaults.go` centralizes many operational defaults in package globals (`UrbitConfig`, `SysConfig`, `WgConfig`) directly tied to runtime composition.
`defaults/version.go` stores the full default version matrix in a giant `DefaultVersionText` JSON literal and unmarshals at init instead of loading through a data artifact boundary.
`config/config.go` merges these defaults with loaded runtime config during `initializeConfig`, so changing defaults requires source edits and rebuild for data updates.
findings.suggestion: Externalize version/config defaults into typed, versioned data files or a small manifest service loader and validate schema at startup, keeping code modules focused on orchestration/policy and minimizing recompilation dependence for environment/version policy updates.

findings.summary: `SupportHandler` couples transport parsing, report assembly, artifact collection, archiving, and submission in one monolithic flow, making behavior changes brittle and hard to reason about.
evidence: `SupportHandler` unmarshals support payloads, creates report directories, orchestrates log collection via `dumpBugReport`, optionally profiles CPU, zips the result, deletes temp artifacts, publishes transition states, and posts the bug report in a single function.
The same module also implements low-level helpers (`dumpBugReport`, `dumpDockerLogs`, `zipDir`, `sendBugReport`) so a single edit touches multiple concerns with strong temporal coupling.
findings.suggestion: Extract a `BugReportCoordinator` that owns request validation and workflow orchestration, and move I/O concerns into smaller collaborators (artifact collector, zip assembler, transport sender, transition emitter) with explicit inputs/outputs. Keep `SupportHandler` as a thin adapter.

findings.summary: The upload endpoint handler intermixes HTTP transport concerns with business workflow, making request handling and import orchestration tightly coupled.
evidence: `HTTPUploadHandler` configures response headers, handles OPTIONS/CORS, request context timeout, validates session headers/path vars, reads multipart chunks, runs pipeline execution, emits status transitions, and writes completion/error HTTP responses.

Pipeline helpers invoked from this function (`validateUploadRequest`, `runImportUploadPipeline`, `persistUploadedChunk`, `combineChunksWithTimeout`, `finalizeUploadOnCompletion`) are still called inline from the same handler, so transport and orchestration are inseparable in practice.
findings.suggestion: Separate request adaptation from orchestration by introducing a `ValidatedUploadRequest` command object and an `ImportUploadService` interface; keep handler focused on mapping HTTP I/O to service calls and status serialization.

findings.summary: Many packages use ad-hoc package-level function vars for dependency seams, creating a fragmented and mutable abstraction style with higher cognitive overhead than needed.
evidence: `system/disk.go` and `system/wifi.go` use mutable function vars (`runDiskCommandFn`, `execCommandForWiFi`, etc.) and exec/call aliases at package level for test seams.

`docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` and `docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go` define broad runtime operation structs/vars that proxy core calls and are overridden in tests, while `config/mc.go` and `shipworkflow/startram.go` add more single-function indirection points.
The same seam pattern is repeated differently across modules instead of a single coherent dependency strategy, which spreads override points and blurs ownership of orchestration boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Replace the ad-hoc global function-var seam pattern with explicit runtime interfaces passed through constructors (or a shared seam/context object) for modules that need replaceable collaborators, and keep per-module seams minimal and uniform.

findings.summary: Broadcast snapshot construction leaks mutable system state maps directly into snapshot output, weakening isolation between data producer and emitter.
evidence: `systemInfoCollector.collect` assigns `sysInfo.Info.SMART = system.SmartResults` directly from a package-level mutable map, rather than cloning at collection time.
`goseg/system/disk.go` maintains `SmartResults` as mutable shared package state, so downstream collectors can observe mutable internal maps without an explicit contract boundary.
findings.suggestion: Introduce immutable snapshot accessors on `system` (for drives/SMART/status data), and clone maps in the collector so emitted broadcast data is detached from mutable package internals.
